@@ -33,7 +33,7 @@ {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-2yd","title":"[ubu.2] Extend state.sh for upgrade tracking","description":"# Extend state.sh for Upgrade Tracking ## Purpose Add upgrade-specific state fields to track multi-reboot upgrade progress. ## State Schema Extensions Add new fields to state.json schema: ```json { "schema_version": 3, // Bump version for new fields // ... existing fields ... // NEW: Ubuntu upgrade tracking "ubuntu_upgrade": { "enabled": true, "started_at": "2025-12-21T10:00:00Z", "original_version": "24.04", "target_version": "25.10", "current_stage": "upgrading", "upgrade_path": ["24.04", "24.10", "25.04", "25.10"], "completed_upgrades": [ {"from": "24.04", "to": "24.10", "completed_at": "..."} ], "current_upgrade": {"from": "24.10", "to": "25.04", "started_at": "..."}, "needs_reboot": false, "resume_after_reboot": true, "last_error": null } } ``` ## Functions to Add ```bash # Initialize upgrade state state_upgrade_init() { local original_version="$1" local target_version="$2" local upgrade_path="$3" # JSON array } # Mark current upgrade as started state_upgrade_start() { local from_version="$1" local to_version="$2" } # Mark current upgrade as completed state_upgrade_complete() { local to_version="$1" } # Mark that system needs reboot state_upgrade_needs_reboot() { # Sets needs_reboot=true, resume_after_reboot=true } # Clear reboot flags after successful resume state_upgrade_resumed() { # Sets needs_reboot=false } # Check if upgrade is complete state_upgrade_is_complete() { # Returns 0 if current version >= target version } # Get current upgrade stage state_upgrade_get_stage() { # Returns: not_started | upgrading | awaiting_reboot | completed } # Record upgrade error state_upgrade_set_error() { local error_msg="$1" } # Clean up upgrade state after completion state_upgrade_cleanup() { # Remove ubuntu_upgrade section entirely } ``` ## Schema Migration Add migration from v2 to v3: - v3 is backwards compatible (new fields are optional) - Existing v2 state files work without modification - ubuntu_upgrade section only added when upgrade starts ## Why Separate State Section? The upgrade state is conceptually different from installation state: - Upgrade happens BEFORE installation - Upgrade may span multiple reboots - Upgrade state should be cleaned up after completion Keeping it in a separate section makes this clear and allows independent cleanup. ## Atomic Write Considerations All state writes already use atomic temp-file+rename pattern. This is critical for upgrade state since we're about to reboot and can't risk corrupted state.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:20:15.998308-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T16:35:14.533749-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T16:35:14.533749-05:00","close_reason":"Extended state.sh with 14 upgrade tracking functions (state_upgrade_init, state_upgrade_start, state_upgrade_complete, etc.). Updated schema to v3 with backward compatibility for v2.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-2yd","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-9xt","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:23:35.258019-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-2yzm","title":"Enhance SSH disconnection recovery with prominent AlertCard","description":"# Task: Enhance SSH Disconnection Recovery Instructions ## Parent Epic [EPIC] Installation Resilience & Progress Feedback (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-vydu) ## Current State run-installer/page.tsx already has: - GuideCaution: "Do not close the terminal window while installation is running" - GuideTip: "If your internet connection drops, just SSH back in and run again" However, this is buried in the SimplerGuide. A more prominent AlertCard near the install command would be more reassuring. ## Enhancement Add a prominent AlertCard near the install command: ```tsx

Do not panic! If your SSH connection drops during installation:

  1. The installer keeps running on the VPS
  2. Just SSH back in using the same command
  3. Run the installer command again — it will resume where it left off

The installer is designed to be run multiple times safely. If anything fails, you can always re-run it.

``` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Prominent AlertCard near the install command (not buried in SimplerGuide) - [ ] Explicitly says "do not panic" - [ ] Explains installer keeps running - [ ] Explains safe to re-run (idempotent) - [ ] Reassuring tone with info variant (blue/calming) ## Note This elevates existing content from SimplerGuide to a more visible location.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:42:20.981302-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T14:04:55.518125-05:00"} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-31e","title":"Installer: implement (or de-scope) cloud/db installs + skip flags","description":"install.sh defines --skip-postgres/--skip-vault/--skip-cloud and smoke test references them, but install.sh never installs PostgreSQL/Vault/cloud CLIs. scripts/lib/cloud_db.sh + acfs.manifest.yaml define these installs, and README claims they're installed.","acceptance_criteria":"Either: - installer runs cloud/db phase (respecting skip flags) without breaking Ubuntu 24.04/25.x CI, OR - docs/manifest updated to match reality and flags removed/hidden.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2025-12-20T14:17:29.066623-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-20T14:37:42.698153-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-20T14:37:42.698153-05:00","close_reason":"Implemented cloud/db phase in install.sh (Postgres 18 via PGDG, Vault via HashiCorp repo, cloud CLIs via bun) honoring skip flags; updated smoke test + phase numbering."} -{"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-322g","title":"Create 'Starting a New Project' guide section","description":"# Task: Create 'Starting a New Project' Guide Section ## Parent Epic [EPIC] Daily Workflow & Getting Started Guide (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-2cvc) ## Description The wizard uses "my-first-project" as an example, but doesn't explain the general pattern for starting real projects. Users need guidance on: - Creating project folders - Initializing git - Setting up with Claude ## Implementation Details Location: apps/web/app/wizard/launch-onboarding/page.tsx or new /learn/new-project page \`\`\`tsx

Starting a New Project

Ready to build something? Here's the pattern:

1. Create a session for your project

This creates a persistent workspace named "my-awesome-app".

2. Create and navigate to a project folder

3. Start Claude and describe your project

Tell Claude what you want to build. For example:

"Create a React app with TypeScript that shows a todo list"
Claude will set up the project structure, install dependencies, and start building. You can guide it step by step or give it the whole vision at once.
\`\`\` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Shows 3-step pattern (session → folder → Claude) - [ ] Uses concrete example project - [ ] Includes example prompt for Claude - [ ] Explains what Claude will do - [ ] Works well on mobile ## UI/UX Notes - Different color from daily workflow to distinguish - Example project should be relatable (todo app is classic) - Emphasize that Claude does the heavy lifting","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:44:00.569413-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T14:20:07.536761-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-322g","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-s4cg","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:57:33.220094-05:00","created_by":"jemanuel"}]} +{"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-322g","title":"Create 'Starting a New Project' guide section","description":"# Task: Create 'Starting a New Project' Guide Section ## Parent Epic [EPIC] Daily Workflow & Getting Started Guide (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-2cvc) ## Description The wizard uses "my-first-project" as an example, but doesn't explain the general pattern for starting real projects. Users need guidance on: - Creating project folders - Initializing git - Setting up with Claude ## Implementation Details Location: apps/web/app/wizard/launch-onboarding/page.tsx or new /learn/new-project page \`\`\`tsx

Starting a New Project

Ready to build something? Here's the pattern:

1. Create a session for your project

This creates a persistent workspace named "my-awesome-app".

2. Create and navigate to a project folder

3. Start Claude and describe your project

Tell Claude what you want to build. For example:

"Create a React app with TypeScript that shows a todo list"
Claude will set up the project structure, install dependencies, and start building. You can guide it step by step or give it the whole vision at once.
\`\`\` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Shows 3-step pattern (session → folder → Claude) - [ ] Uses concrete example project - [ ] Includes example prompt for Claude - [ ] Explains what Claude will do - [ ] Works well on mobile ## UI/UX Notes - Different color from daily workflow to distinguish - Example project should be relatable (todo app is classic) - Emphasize that Claude does the heavy lifting","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:44:00.569413-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T14:21:33.434828-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-22T14:21:33.434828-05:00","close_reason":"Implemented 'Starting a New Project' guide with 3-step pattern (session → folder → Claude), concrete example, and Claude explanation","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-322g","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-s4cg","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:57:33.220094-05:00","created_by":"jemanuel"}]} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-325","title":"Implement agent auth functional tests","description":"# Task: Implement agent auth functional tests ## Context Part of EPIC: Enhanced Doctor with Functional Tests (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-t9i) ## What to Do Create functional tests for AI agent authentication: ### check_claude_auth() 1. Check if claude binary exists (skip if not) 2. Check if ~/.claude/config.json exists 3. Try claude --print-system-info (low-cost API check) 4. Return: 0=OK, 1=fail, 2=skip ### check_codex_auth() 1. Check if codex binary exists 2. Check for OPENAI_API_KEY env var 3. Check for API key in ~/.zshrc.local 4. Return status ### check_gemini_auth() 1. Check if gemini binary exists 2. Check for GOOGLE_API_KEY or GEMINI_API_KEY 3. Check for credentials file 4. Return status ## Acceptance Criteria - Each check has clear pass/fail criteria - Skipped if tool not installed (status 2) - Auth issues detected reliably - No false positives ## Files to Modify - scripts/lib/doctor.sh: Add check functions","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T12:45:18.598224-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T14:57:34.018612-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T14:57:34.018612-05:00","close_reason":"Implemented check_claude_auth(), check_codex_auth(), check_gemini_auth() with config/API key checks","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-325","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-01s","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T12:47:34.958137-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-32g","title":"[ubu.9] Handle LTS-to-LTS upgrade path special case","description":"# Handle LTS-to-LTS Upgrade Path Special Case ## Purpose Ubuntu LTS releases can upgrade directly to the next LTS (22.04 → 24.04) OR follow the regular path through intermediate releases. We need to handle this correctly. ## Background Ubuntu upgrade paths have special rules: - **Normal releases**: Must upgrade sequentially (24.10 → 25.04 → 25.10) - **LTS releases**: Can upgrade directly to next LTS (22.04 → 24.04) - **LTS to normal**: Requires enabling "Prompt=normal" in /etc/update-manager/release-upgrades ## Default Behavior By default, do-release-upgrade on LTS systems only shows LTS upgrades. To see non-LTS upgrades, need to modify configuration: ```bash # In /etc/update-manager/release-upgrades # Change: Prompt=lts # To: Prompt=normal ``` ## Implementation ### Detection ```bash # Check if current system is LTS ubuntu_is_lts() { local version=$(ubuntu_get_version_string) # LTS versions: 22.04, 24.04, 26.04, etc. [[ "$version" =~ ^[0-9]+\.04$ ]] } ``` ### Path Calculation for LTS Systems ```bash ubuntu_calculate_upgrade_path() { local target="$1" local current=$(ubuntu_get_version_string) if ubuntu_is_lts; then # Check if we should go LTS-to-LTS or through normal releases local next_lts=$(ubuntu_get_next_lts "$current") if ubuntu_version_gte "$next_lts" "$target"; then # Target is the next LTS or earlier - go direct echo "$next_lts" else # Target is beyond next LTS - need to go through normal releases # Enable normal release channel ubuntu_enable_normal_releases ubuntu_calculate_sequential_path "$current" "$target" fi else ubuntu_calculate_sequential_path "$current" "$target" fi } ``` ### Enable Normal Releases ```bash ubuntu_enable_normal_releases() { local config="/etc/update-manager/release-upgrades" if [[ -f "$config" ]]; then # Backup original cp "$config" "${config}.acfs-backup" # Change Prompt=lts to Prompt=normal sed -i "s/^Prompt=lts$/Prompt=normal/" "$config" log_info "Enabled normal release upgrades (was LTS-only)" fi } ``` ## Example Upgrade Paths ### From 22.04 LTS to 25.10 Option A (LTS path first): - 22.04 → 24.04 (LTS hop) - 24.04 → 24.10 → 25.04 → 25.10 (normal path) Option B (all normal): - 22.04 → 22.10 → 23.04 → 23.10 → 24.04 → 24.10 → 25.04 → 25.10 **We choose Option A** - fewer hops, more stable. ### From 24.04 LTS to 25.10 - 24.04 → 24.10 → 25.04 → 25.10 (normal path, 3 hops) ## Configuration Restoration After reaching target version, restore original LTS-only setting: ```bash ubuntu_restore_lts_only() { local config="/etc/update-manager/release-upgrades" local backup="${config}.acfs-backup" if [[ -f "$backup" ]]; then mv "$backup" "$config" log_info "Restored LTS-only release setting" fi } ``` ## Testing Notes - Test from 22.04: Verify LTS-to-LTS hop works - Test from 24.04: Verify normal path enables correctly - Test config restoration after completion","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:24:05.237039-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T16:39:24.893274-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T16:39:24.893274-05:00","close_reason":"Added LTS detection (ubuntu_is_lts - even years only), next LTS calculation (ubuntu_get_next_lts), and release channel management (ubuntu_enable_normal_releases, ubuntu_restore_lts_only).","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-32g","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-9xt","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:24:40.623353-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-367d","title":"Audit and expand Jargon component terms","description":"# Task: Audit and Expand Jargon Component Terms ## Parent Epic [EPIC] Jargon System & Documentation Polish (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-7i45) ## Description The Jargon component provides tooltip definitions for technical terms, but coverage is incomplete and some definitions are too technical. Need a comprehensive audit. ## Implementation Details Location: apps/web/components/jargon.tsx ### Terms to audit/add: - VPS - Virtual Private Server (already exists, verify quality) - SSH - Secure Shell (verify quality) - Terminal - text interface (verify quality) - Command line - same as terminal (alias?) - Root - superuser/admin account - sudo - run as superuser - Ubuntu user - regular user account - tmux - terminal multiplexer - Session - persistent terminal environment - Repository/Repo - code storage folder - Git - version control system - API key - authentication token - Environment variable - system setting - Port - network endpoint - IP address - network address - zsh - shell program - Bash - shell program ### Quality criteria for definitions: - No jargon in the definition itself - One sentence maximum for tooltip - Relatable analogy if possible - Consistent tone ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] All terms listed above have definitions - [ ] Definitions are beginner-friendly (no technical jargon) - [ ] Tooltip text fits comfortably (not too long) - [ ] All wizard pages use Jargon wrappers appropriately - [ ] Mobile tooltip experience is good (tap to show) ## UI/UX Notes - Tooltips should be quick reads (2-3 seconds) - Consider linking to glossary for longer explanations - Test on mobile — touch targets and positioning","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:44:46.425151-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T13:44:46.425151-05:00"} @@ -79,7 +79,7 @@ {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-5zm","title":"Implement report_failure() with structured output","description":"# Task: Implement report_failure() with structured output ## Context Part of EPIC: Per-Phase Error Reporting (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-fkf) ## What to Do Create a comprehensive failure report function: ### Human Output (terminal) ``` ╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗ ║ ✖ INSTALLATION FAILED ║ ║ ║ ║ Phase 6/10: Language Runtimes ║ ║ Failed at: Installing Rust via rustup ║ ║ ║ ║ Error: curl: (7) Failed to connect to sh.rustup.rs ║ ║ ║ ║ Suggested Fix: ║ ║ 1. Check internet: curl -I https://google.com ║ ║ 2. Retry: curl ... | bash -s -- --resume ║ ║ ║ ║ Full log: /var/log/acfs/install.log ║ ╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝ ``` ### JSON Output (log file) Append structured JSON to install.log for tooling. ### With gum Use gum style for prettier box drawing. ## Acceptance Criteria - Shows phase number and name - Shows specific step that failed - Shows error excerpt (truncated if long) - Shows suggested fix from ERROR_PATTERNS - Shows resume command - Works with and without gum ## Files to Modify - install.sh: Add report_failure() function","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T12:44:00.671428-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T14:47:14.92113-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T14:47:14.92113-05:00","close_reason":"Created scripts/lib/report.sh with report_failure(), report_success(), report_warning(), report_skipped_summary(). Supports gum styling, JSON logging, and suggested fixes from ERROR_PATTERNS.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-5zm","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-vwv","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T12:47:30.834519-05:00","created_by":"daemon"},{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-5zm","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-qqo","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T12:47:31.011809-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-5zt","title":"Design state.json v2 schema","description":"# Task: Design state.json v2 schema ## Context Part of EPIC: Phase-Granular Progress Persistence ## Critical Design Decision: Track Phase IDs, Not Numbers **Problem with tracking phase numbers:** If we store `completed_phases: [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]` and later ACFS reorders phases or adds a new phase 3, the resume logic would skip the wrong phases. **Solution:** Track phase IDs (stable identifiers): ```json { "schema_version": 2, "completed_phases": ["user_setup", "apt_packages", "shell_setup", "cli_tools", "languages"], "current_phase": "agents", ... } ``` ## Full Schema ```json { "schema_version": 2, "version": "0.1.0", "mode": "vibe", "started_at": "2025-01-15T10:30:00Z", "last_updated": "2025-01-15T10:42:00Z", "completed_phases": ["user_setup", "apt_packages", "shell_setup"], "current_phase": "cli_tools", "current_step": "Installing ripgrep", "failed_phase": null, "failed_step": null, "skipped_tools": ["ntm", "bv"], "skipped_phases": ["postgres", "vault"], "phase_durations": { "user_setup": 12, "apt_packages": 45 } } ``` ## Phase IDs (stable, never change meaning) - user_setup - apt_packages - shell_setup - cli_tools - languages - tools - database - cloud - agents - stack - finalize ## Backwards Compatibility - If schema_version missing or 1: treat as legacy, offer fresh start - Phase IDs are strings, won't conflict with old number-based format ## Acceptance Criteria - Schema uses phase IDs not numbers - Documented in code comments - Backwards compatible with v1 detection - All fields have sensible defaults","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T12:42:39.486596-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T14:39:43.137058-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T14:39:43.137058-05:00","close_reason":"Already implemented in scripts/lib/state.sh. Schema v2 uses phase IDs, is documented, and has backwards compatibility."} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-6fx","title":"cloud_db.sh: verify respects SKIP flags + optional installs","description":"scripts/lib/cloud_db.sh claims to respect SKIP_POSTGRES/SKIP_VAULT/SKIP_CLOUD in install_all_cloud_db, but verify_cloud_db still fails if those tools are intentionally skipped.","acceptance_criteria":"- With SKIP_POSTGRES=true, verify_cloud_db does not mark psql missing as failure. - With SKIP_VAULT=true, verify_cloud_db does not mark vault missing as failure. - With SKIP_CLOUD=true, verify_cloud_db does not mark cloud CLIs missing as failure.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2025-12-20T14:45:03.242319-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-20T14:52:41.60688-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-20T14:52:41.60688-05:00","close_reason":"Updated verify_cloud_db to honor SKIP_POSTGRES/SKIP_VAULT/SKIP_CLOUD so skips don't count as failures."} -{"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-6r9o","title":"Enhance terminal copy/paste instructions with dedicated section","description":"# Task: Enhance Terminal Copy/Paste Instructions ## Parent Epic [EPIC] Terminal Onboarding Foundation (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-ux1f) ## Current State Copy/paste instructions currently exist in: - generate-ssh-key/page.tsx Step 3 (Mac and Windows paste shortcuts) - ssh-connect/page.tsx Step 3 (same) However, users encounter copy/paste FIRST in install-terminal when downloading, so a dedicated section there would help. ## Enhancement Add a dedicated GuideSection in install-terminal/page.tsx SimplerGuide with comprehensive copy/paste instructions: ```tsx

Terminal copy/paste works a bit differently than other apps:

Mac (Ghostty/WezTerm)

  • ⌘ + C to copy
  • ⌘ + V to paste
  • Works just like other Mac apps!

Windows (Windows Terminal)

  • Right-click to paste (easiest!)
  • Or use Ctrl + Shift + C/V
  • Note: Ctrl + C STOPS commands, it does not copy!
 ``` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Consolidates copy/paste instructions in install-terminal (first encounter) - [ ] Mac instructions are clear and simple - [ ] Windows instructions warn about Ctrl+C danger - [ ] Uses kbd styling for key combinations - [ ] OS-conditional display (show only relevant OS) ## Note This enhances existing scattered mentions with a dedicated, comprehensive section.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:39:41.923436-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T14:20:59.889095-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-22T14:20:59.889095-05:00","close_reason":"Already implemented in TerminalBasicsSection component with: OS-specific copy/paste AlertCards, kbd styling for keyboard shortcuts, Windows Ctrl+C warning. Section appears in install-terminal page at first terminal encounter."} +{"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-6r9o","title":"Enhance terminal copy/paste instructions with dedicated section","description":"# Task: Enhance Terminal Copy/Paste Instructions ## Parent Epic [EPIC] Terminal Onboarding Foundation (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-ux1f) ## Current State Copy/paste instructions currently exist in: - generate-ssh-key/page.tsx Step 3 (Mac and Windows paste shortcuts) - ssh-connect/page.tsx Step 3 (same) However, users encounter copy/paste FIRST in install-terminal when downloading, so a dedicated section there would help. ## Enhancement Add a dedicated GuideSection in install-terminal/page.tsx SimplerGuide with comprehensive copy/paste instructions: ```tsx

Terminal copy/paste works a bit differently than other apps:

Mac (Ghostty/WezTerm)

  • ⌘ + C to copy
  • ⌘ + V to paste
  • Works just like other Mac apps!

Windows (Windows Terminal)

  • Right-click to paste (easiest!)
  • Or use Ctrl + Shift + C/V
  • Note: Ctrl + C STOPS commands, it does not copy!
 ``` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Consolidates copy/paste instructions in install-terminal (first encounter) - [ ] Mac instructions are clear and simple - [ ] Windows instructions warn about Ctrl+C danger - [ ] Uses kbd styling for key combinations - [ ] OS-conditional display (show only relevant OS) ## Note This enhances existing scattered mentions with a dedicated, comprehensive section.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:39:41.923436-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T14:21:11.767553-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-22T14:21:11.767553-05:00","close_reason":"Already implemented: TerminalBasicsSection in install-terminal/page.tsx has dedicated copy/paste section with Mac (cmd+C/V) and Windows (right-click, Ctrl+Shift+V) instructions, including Ctrl+C warning for Windows"} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-6w9u","title":"Create dedicated /glossary page","description":"# Task: Create Dedicated Glossary Page ## Parent Epic [EPIC] Jargon System & Documentation Polish (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-7i45) ## Description Create a comprehensive, searchable glossary page where users can look up technical terms. This provides a deeper reference than tooltips and helps users who want to understand the concepts more fully. ## Implementation Details Location: apps/web/app/glossary/page.tsx \`\`\`tsx // Key features: // 1. Alphabetical listing of all terms // 2. Search/filter functionality // 3. Categorization (Shell, Networking, Tools, etc.) // 4. Detailed explanations (more than tooltip) // 5. Links to relevant wizard steps or lessons interface GlossaryEntry { term: string; short: string; // Tooltip version long: string; // Full explanation category: 'shell' | 'networking' | 'tools' | 'concepts'; relatedTerms?: string[]; learnMore?: string; // Link to lesson } \`\`\` Page should include: - Search box at top - Category filters - Clean, scannable layout - Mobile-friendly (single column) - Links from jargon tooltips to glossary entries ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] All terms from Jargon component included - [ ] Search filters results in real-time - [ ] Categories help organize terms - [ ] Long explanations are thorough but accessible - [ ] Links to related wizard steps where applicable - [ ] Mobile-responsive design ## UI/UX Notes - This is a reference page, not a learning page - Users might land here from Google searches - Consider SEO implications (good meta descriptions) - Use same visual style as rest of site","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:45:10.252228-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T13:45:10.252228-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-6w9u","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-367d","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:57:43.702732-05:00","created_by":"jemanuel"}]} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-6y4w","title":"TASK: Implement prompt_ssh_key() function in user.sh","description":"## Parent Feature agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-fslp (Installer SSH Key Integration) ## Summary Create the \`prompt_ssh_key()\` function that interactively prompts users to paste their SSH public key and installs it to /root/.ssh/authorized_keys. ## Implementation Details ### File Location \`scripts/lib/user.sh\` - add after existing functions ### Function Specification \`\`\`bash # Prompt user for SSH public key and install it # Called when running as root with no existing key # Returns 0 on success or skip, 1 on invalid key prompt_ssh_key() { # 1. Check if we already have a valid key if [[ -f /root/.ssh/authorized_keys ]]; then if grep -q "^ssh-" /root/.ssh/authorized_keys 2>/dev/null; then log_detail "SSH key already present, skipping prompt" return 0 fi fi # 2. Check if stdin is a terminal (interactive mode) if [[ ! -t 0 ]]; then log_warn "Non-interactive mode detected, skipping SSH key prompt" log_detail "You can add your key later with: ssh-copy-id" return 0 fi # 3. Display prompt UI echo "" echo "╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗" echo "║ SSH Key Setup ║" echo "╠══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣" echo "║ Let's set up SSH key authentication so you won't need ║" echo "║ to enter a password every time you connect. ║" echo "╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝" echo "" echo "Your public key should start with:" echo " ssh-ed25519 AAAAC3NzaC1..." OR ssh-rsa AAAAB3NzaC1..." echo "" echo "You saved this earlier when you ran ssh-keygen on your computer." echo "(Press Enter to skip - you'll need password for future logins)" echo "" # 4. Read the key read -r -p "Paste your public key: " pubkey # 5. Handle skip if [[ -z "\$pubkey" ]]; then log_warn "SSH key setup skipped" log_detail "You can add your key later by running:" log_detail " echo 'your-key-here' >> ~/.ssh/authorized_keys" return 0 fi # 6. Validate key format if [[ ! "\$pubkey" =~ ^ssh-(ed25519|rsa|ecdsa|dss)[[:space:]] ]]; then log_error "Invalid SSH key format" log_detail "Expected format: ssh-ed25519 AAAA... or ssh-rsa AAAA..." log_detail "Make sure you copied the PUBLIC key (the .pub file)" return 1 fi # 7. Install the key mkdir -p /root/.ssh chmod 700 /root/.ssh echo "\$pubkey" >> /root/.ssh/authorized_keys chmod 600 /root/.ssh/authorized_keys log_success "SSH key installed successfully" log_detail "You can now connect with: ssh -i ~/.ssh/your_key root@this_ip" return 0 } \`\`\` ### Edge Cases to Handle 1. **Existing key**: Skip prompt if authorized_keys already has valid keys 2. **Non-interactive**: Skip if stdin is not a terminal (curl|bash mode) 3. **Empty input**: Treat as skip, show warning 4. **Invalid format**: Show helpful error, don't install 5. **Permission issues**: Create .ssh dir if missing, set proper perms ### Testing Scenarios 1. Fresh VPS, password-only → should prompt 2. VPS with existing key → should skip 3. Paste valid ed25519 key → should install 4. Paste valid rsa key → should install 5. Paste invalid text → should error 6. Press Enter (skip) → should warn and continue 7. Run via curl|bash → should skip gracefully ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Function exists in user.sh - [ ] Validates key format before installing - [ ] Handles all edge cases gracefully - [ ] Creates .ssh directory if missing - [ ] Sets correct permissions (700 for dir, 600 for file) - [ ] Works when run multiple times (idempotent) - [ ] Clear success/error messages ## Files Modified - scripts/lib/user.sh (add function)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T19:19:06.013332-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T19:31:59.551752-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T19:31:59.551752-05:00","close_reason":"Function already implemented in scripts/lib/user.sh lines 193-257. Handles existing key check, interactive mode detection, key validation, and proper permissions.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-6y4w","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-fslp","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T19:19:25.962058-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-75e","title":"Core infrastructure for acfs-update","description":"## What Create the main acfs-update script skeleton with: - Argument parsing (categories, --yes, --dry-run, --verbose, --quiet, --continue/--abort-on-failure) - Logging infrastructure (to ~/.acfs/logs/updates/YYYY-MM-DD-HHMMSS.log) - Progress display and summary generation - Helper functions for version detection, before/after capture - Checksum verification helpers (for stack updates) ## Why All category-specific update implementations depend on this foundation. Must be solid and well-tested before building on top. ## IMPORTANT: Execution Order Categories must run in a specific order at runtime (even if beads are implemented in parallel): 1. system - apt updates may affect Python, libraries 2. runtimes - bun, rust, uv depend on system packages 3. shell - may use runtimes 4. agents - use runtimes (bun for codex/gemini) 5. stack - use runtimes (uv for mcp_agent_mail, cargo for some tools) 6. cloud - use runtimes (bun for most CLIs) ## Script Structure - Main entry point with arg parsing - Category dispatch (run selected categories in order) - Per-tool update wrapper with logging - Summary generation at end ## Considerations - Script location: ~/.acfs/bin/acfs-update (added to PATH during install) - Logging must capture stdout/stderr from each update - Version detection varies by tool (--version, git describe, etc.) - For --dry-run: show what WOULD happen without doing it - For --yes: suppress all prompts, proceed automatically ## Success Criteria - [ ] acfs-update --help shows usage - [ ] --dry-run mode works (no actual changes) - [ ] Log file created with timestamps - [ ] Summary shows "X updated, Y skipped, Z failed" - [ ] Categories run in correct order","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2025-12-21T13:25:14.89257-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T15:08:16.127634-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T15:08:16.127634-05:00","close_reason":"Implemented core update infrastructure: logging, version detection, new CLI flags, abort-on-failure support","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-75e","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-x0l","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T13:25:20.750551-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]} @@ -269,7 +269,7 @@ {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-ua5","title":"EPIC: Fix Codex CLI Authentication Documentation (OAuth, Not API Key)","description":"## Overview This epic addresses a significant documentation error across the ACFS codebase: the incorrect assumption that OpenAI's codex-cli uses an OPENAI_API_KEY environment variable for authentication. ## The Reality OpenAI's codex-cli uses a **built-in OAuth authentication system**, similar to how Claude Code and Gemini CLI work: 1. **Authentication Method**: OAuth via web browser or device-level code flow 2. **Accounts**: Uses ChatGPT Pro/Plus accounts (consumer accounts), NOT API accounts 3. **No API Key Needed**: OPENAI_API_KEY is for the OpenAI API, NOT for codex-cli 4. **Security Setting Required**: Must enable device code flow in ChatGPT settings (Settings → Security → API/Device access) ## Why This Matters 1. **User Confusion**: Current docs tell users to set OPENAI_API_KEY, which is: - Wrong (codex-cli ignores it) - Misleading (users may think they need an OpenAI API account) - Potentially dangerous (exposing API keys unnecessarily) 2. **Doctor Checks Broken**: Our health checks look for OPENAI_API_KEY, which means: - Legitimate users (authenticated via OAuth) will get false warnings - Users setting OPENAI_API_KEY will get false positives 3. **Account Confusion**: OpenAI has TWO account types: - **API Account**: Uses OPENAI_API_KEY, billed separately, for programmatic access - **ChatGPT Account** (Pro/Plus): Uses OAuth, consumer subscription, what codex-cli uses ## Success Criteria 1. No references to OPENAI_API_KEY for codex-cli authentication 2. Doctor checks correctly verify codex OAuth token 3. All documentation accurately describes the OAuth flow 4. Clear distinction between API accounts and ChatGPT accounts 5. Install messages guide users to run codex auth","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:24:54.207594-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T16:46:17.599689-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T16:46:17.599689-05:00","close_reason":"All 8 subtasks completed: doctor.sh, agents.sh, README, onboard lesson, web app audited. No more incorrect OPENAI_API_KEY references for codex-cli."} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-uga","title":"stack installers: respect non-interactive mode + refine gemini auth check","description":"Avoid forcing --yes flags for stack installers when running interactively, and refine onboarding gemini auth detection to rely on CLI credential files rather than API keys. Also ensure contract validation includes _acfs_is_interactive.","acceptance_criteria":"- stack.sh only passes --yes automatically in non-interactive runs\n- authChecks gemini detection matches actual CLI credential storage\n- contract.sh verifies _acfs_is_interactive exists","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2025-12-21T18:03:00.832225-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T18:03:16.820162-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T18:03:16.820162-05:00","close_reason":"Updated stack installers to add --yes only when non-interactive; refined gemini auth check + tests; ensured contract validates _acfs_is_interactive."} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-umqa","title":"EPIC: Beginner UX Overhaul v1.0 - Password-First SSH Flow","description":"## Overview This epic addresses the critical UX issues identified in STEP_BY_STEP_AUDIT_OF_USER_EXPERIENCE.md that cause beginner users to abandon the setup process. The core innovation is switching from "SSH key during VPS creation" to "password-first connection with installer-managed key setup." ## The Core Problem The current flow requires users to paste their SSH public key into the VPS provider's UI during server creation. This fails because: 1. **Provider UI Variance**: Contabo, OVH, DigitalOcean, etc. all have different interfaces 2. **Hidden SSH Key Fields**: Users can't find where to paste the key 3. **Timing Issues**: Some providers require key during creation, others after 4. **No Verification**: If the key is pasted wrong, user gets cryptic "Permission denied" errors 5. **Brittle**: Any change to provider UI breaks our instructions ## The Solution: Password-First Flow Instead of relying on provider UIs, we use a universal approach: 1. **User creates VPS with password** (every provider supports this reliably) 2. **User SSHs as root with password**: `ssh root@` (standard, no special keys) 3. **Installer prompts for SSH public key** (we control this experience) 4. **Installer installs key and migrates to ubuntu user** (existing migrate_ssh_keys works) 5. **User verifies key-based connection** (closes loop, proves it works) ## Why This Is Better - **Universal**: Works with ANY VPS provider without specific instructions - **Controlled**: We own the key installation process, not the provider - **Verifiable**: We can validate the key and confirm it works - **Recoverable**: If something goes wrong, user is still connected and can retry - **Simpler**: "Just pick Ubuntu and get a password" is easier than "find the SSH key section" ## Revised User Journey ### Phase 1: Local Setup (unchanged) 1. Choose OS 2. Install terminal 3. Generate SSH key → **SAVE TO NOTES** (emphasized) ### Phase 2: VPS Creation (simplified) 4. Rent VPS (pick provider/plan) 5. Create VPS → **Ubuntu + root password** (skip SSH key entirely) ### Phase 3: First Connection (new approach) 6. SSH as root with password → `ssh root@` 7. Run installer → **Installer prompts for SSH key** 8. Verify key connection → Disconnect, reconnect with key ### Phase 4: Post-Setup (enhanced) 9. Essential accounts only (GitHub, Claude) 10. "Your First 5 Minutes" guided walkthrough 11. Reconnection instructions + SSH config setup ## Success Metrics - **Completion Rate**: Track % of users who reach launch-onboarding - **SSH Error Rate**: Reduce "Permission denied" support requests - **Time to First Success**: Measure time from start to first Claude interaction ## Technical Scope This epic includes: - Installer modifications for SSH key prompting - Wizard page updates for password-first flow - Accounts page simplification - Post-installation onboarding enhancements - Installer UX improvements (progress indicators) - Documentation updates ## Dependencies None - this is a foundational epic. ## Risks & Mitigations | Risk | Mitigation | |------|------------| | Password auth less secure than key-only | Key is installed immediately during first session | | User forgets password | Password is only needed once, then key takes over | | Some providers don't give root access | Fallback to ubuntu user with password works similarly | | Pasting long key in terminal fails | ED25519 keys are short (~100 chars), add validation | ## Estimated Effort - Installer changes: 4-6 hours - Wizard page updates: 8-12 hours - Post-installation UX: 6-8 hours - Testing across providers: 4-6 hours - Documentation: 4-6 hours **Total: ~30-40 hours of focused work**","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2025-12-21T19:14:44.321974-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T19:29:43.625364-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T19:29:43.625364-05:00","close_reason":"Design document is complete. The EPIC describes the full password-first SSH flow approach. All child features and tasks have detailed specs. Closing to unblock downstream implementation work."} -{"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-unqt","title":"Enhance SSH key prompt guidance with OutputPreview","description":"# Task: Enhance SSH Key Prompt Guidance ## Parent Epic [EPIC] SSH Key Generation Clarity (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-1wk6) ## Current State generate-ssh-key/page.tsx already has Step 4 "Answer the prompts" explaining: - First prompt: "Enter passphrase" → Just press Enter (leave empty) - Second prompt: "Enter same passphrase again" → Press Enter again However, the current guidance is text-only. They don't. When a complete beginner downloads and opens Ghostty/WezTerm, they see a blank screen with a blinking cursor and have NO IDEA what to do. This is the #1 reason beginners abandon the setup process. ## Background & Reasoning The terminal is the foundational interface for everything in ACFS. Every subsequent step (SSH key generation, SSH connection, running the installer, using agents) requires terminal proficiency. Without this foundation, users cannot proceed. Currently we tell users to "install a terminal" and immediately jump to "run this SSH command." This assumes knowledge of: - What a prompt is ($ or %) - That you type after the prompt - How to copy/paste in a terminal (different from regular apps!) - How to press Enter to execute - How to recognize success vs failure ## User Story As a complete beginner who has never used a command line, I want to understand the basics of terminal interaction, So that I can confidently execute the commands in the wizard. ## Success Criteria 1. User can open terminal and identify the prompt 2. User can type a simple command and press Enter 3. User can copy a command from the wizard and paste it into terminal 4. User can recognize when a command succeeded vs failed 5. User feels confident, not scared, when looking at the terminal ## Scope - Add "Terminal Basics" micro-lesson after terminal installation step - Include interactive verification (type echo hello, see hello) - Explain copy/paste differences by OS - Explain the prompt ($ vs %) and what it means - Visual examples with screenshots or animations ## Dependencies None - this is the foundational epic that other improvements depend on. ## Blocks - SSH Key Generation improvements - SSH Connection improvements - All subsequent terminal-based steps ## Technical Approach Add a new wizard step or expand the "Install Terminal" step to include a "Try Your First Commands" section with: - SimplerGuide component explaining prompts - Interactive checklist with verification commands - Copy/paste instructions per OS - Success state showing "You're ready to continue!" ## Estimated Effort Medium (2-3 wizard page updates, new content) ## Priority Justification P0 (Critical) - Without this, all other wizard improvements are moot. Users can't get past step 3 if they don't understand terminals.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:33:28.755861-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T14:20:17.315849-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-22T14:20:17.315849-05:00","close_reason":"Implemented Terminal Basics section with: 1) Prompt explanation ($ % > symbols), 2) OS-specific copy/paste instructions, 3) Interactive 'echo hello' verification with OutputPreview, 4) Success confirmation. Added TerminalBasicsSection component to install-terminal page."} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-uxc","title":"Implement init_state() and save_state() functions","description":"# Task: Implement init_state() and save_state() functions ## Context Part of EPIC: Phase-Granular Progress Persistence (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-5uu) ## What to Do Create bash functions for state management: ### init_state() - Check if ~/.acfs/state.json exists - If exists: load COMPLETED_PHASES array, CURRENT_PHASE, detect resume scenario - If not exists: initialize empty state, create ~/.acfs directory - Handle corrupted state files gracefully (warn and start fresh) ### save_state() - Write current state to ~/.acfs/state.json atomically - Use write-to-temp-then-move pattern to prevent corruption - Include all tracked fields from v2 schema - Log state save for debugging ## Acceptance Criteria - init_state() correctly loads or initializes state - save_state() writes atomic JSON (no partial writes) - Corrupted state file detected and handled - Functions work when jq is not installed (use sed fallback) ## Implementation Notes - Use mktemp for atomic writes - Consider that jq may not be installed yet (use basic parsing) - Test both fresh install and resume scenarios ## Files to Modify - install.sh or scripts/lib/state.sh (new)","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T12:42:40.635486-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T14:38:29.61414-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T14:38:29.61414-05:00","close_reason":"Implemented in scripts/lib/state.sh with v2 schema, atomic writes, and full lifecycle functions","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-uxc","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-5zt","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T12:47:27.650646-05:00","created_by":"daemon"},{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-uxc","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-aa1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T12:51:28.454984-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-v70e","title":"TASK: Add service tier field and reorganize accounts page","description":"## Parent Feature agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-rn2q (Accounts Page Simplification) ## Summary Add a \`tier\` field to the Service type in lib/services.ts and reorganize the accounts page to show services in three collapsible sections: Essential, Recommended, Optional. ## Implementation Details ### 1. Update lib/services.ts Add tier field to Service interface: \`\`\`typescript export type ServiceTier = 'essential' | 'recommended' | 'optional'; export interface Service { // ... existing fields tier: ServiceTier; } \`\`\` Assign tiers to each service: \`\`\`typescript // Essential (2 services) { name: "GitHub", tier: "essential", ... }, { name: "Claude", tier: "essential", ... }, // Recommended (2-3 services) { name: "OpenAI", tier: "recommended", ... }, { name: "Google AI", tier: "recommended", ... }, // Optional (everything else) { name: "Vercel", tier: "optional", ... }, { name: "Cloudflare", tier: "optional", ... }, { name: "Supabase", tier: "optional", ... }, // etc. \`\`\` ### 2. Create groupByTier helper \`\`\`typescript export function groupByTier(): Record { const groups: Record = { essential: [], recommended: [], optional: [], }; for (const service of SERVICES) { groups[service.tier].push(service); } return groups; } \`\`\` ### 3. Update accounts page layout \`\`\`tsx export default function AccountsPage() { const groups = groupByTier(); return (
{/* Essential - always expanded */}

Essential Accounts (do these now)

You need these to start using the system.

{groups.essential.map(service => ( ))}
{/* Recommended - collapsed by default */}

Recommended (after your first project)

 {groups.recommended.map(...)} {/* Optional - collapsed by default */}

Optional (when you need them)

 {groups.optional.map(...)}
); } \`\`\` ### 4. Add Collapsible component (if not exists) Use shadcn/ui Collapsible or create simple one with state. ## Tier Assignments | Service | Tier | Rationale | |---------|------|-----------| | GitHub | essential | Code backup, critical | | Claude/Anthropic | essential | Primary AI agent | | OpenAI | recommended | For Codex CLI | | Google AI | recommended | For Gemini CLI | | Vercel | optional | Only for web deployment | | Cloudflare | optional | Only for custom domains | | Supabase | optional | Only for databases | | Docker Hub | optional | Only for containers | ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Service type has tier field - [ ] All services assigned appropriate tier - [ ] groupByTier helper works - [ ] Essential section always visible - [ ] Recommended/Optional collapsed by default - [ ] Clear messaging for each tier - [ ] Existing functionality preserved ## Dependencies - None (standalone refactor) ## Files Modified - apps/web/lib/services.ts - apps/web/app/wizard/accounts/page.tsx","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T19:22:24.495829-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T19:40:26.293691-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T19:40:26.293691-05:00","close_reason":"Services.ts tier field and accounts page tier sections already implemented by other agent.
